title: Apple turnovers
subtitle: Rough puff pastry folded over spiced apple, glazed and baked
excerpt: Eight apple turnovers made from a 500 g batch of rough puff pastry filled with cinnamon-spiced Bramley apple. The rough puff is made here rather than shop-bought, the lamination gives them the flaky layers that ready-rolled doesn't achieve at home.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the turnover pastry chapter documents half-moon or triangular shortcrust or puff pastry cases filled with sweetened stewed fruit. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton documents rough puff pastry (butter worked into the dough in larger pieces than shortcrust, producing a laminated result without the full butter block of classic puff) as a practical household alternative to classic puff. The apple filling with cinnamon and sugar is documented across British domestic baking literature from Beeton onward.

content.text: A good apple turnover requires pastry that actually flakes. The

here takes longer than opening a packet of ready-rolled but the layers are visibly different, thicker, with open honeycombed flakes where the butter has steamed during baking. The filling is straightforward Bramley apple, briefly cooked with cinnamon and sugar, cooled before it goes into the pastry.

attrs.body: Rough puff pastry depends on cold butter staying in discrete pieces through the rolling-and-folding process. If the kitchen is warm, put the dough in the fridge for 20 minutes between each fold. The butter smearing into the dough (instead of staying in flakes) is what produces a short, crumbly result rather than flaky layers.
attrs.title: Keep everything cold

items.name: Bramley apple
items.prepNote: about 600 g, peeled, cored, and diced into 1 cm pieces

items.name: Cornflour
items.prepNote: 1 tablespoon; thickens the juice so it doesn't make the pastry soggy

content.text: Combine the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter dice and toss to coat in flour. Work quickly with your fingertips, squeezing the butter pieces flat without rubbing them in. You want flat, pea-sized pieces of butter coated in flour, not the fine breadcrumb texture of shortcrust. Add the {{water}} ice cold water and cut through the mixture with a round-bladed knife until it begins to clump. Press into a rough rectangle, wrap, and refrigerate for 20 minutes.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough into a rectangle about 30 × 20 cm. Fold the top third down and the bottom third up, like a letter. Rotate 90°. This is one fold. Wrap and return to the fridge for 20 minutes.

Repeat the roll and fold twice more, chilling for 20 minutes after each fold. After three folds, you should see no visible butter pieces and the dough should look streaky (not uniform), the streaks are the layers. After the final fold, wrap and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before rolling out.

Toss the diced {{apple-bramley}} Bramley apple with {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar, {{cinnamon-ground}} cinnamon, and {{cornflour}}. Cook in a small saucepan over medium heat for 5 to 8 minutes, stirring, until the apple has softened slightly and the juices have thickened around it. The apple should hold its shape but no longer be raw. Spread on a plate and leave to cool completely. Do not fill the pastry with hot filling.

Roll the chilled pastry to about 3 to 4 mm thick and cut into 8 squares of roughly 12 × 12 cm. Place a spoonful of cold apple filling on one half of each square, leaving a 1.5 cm border. Brush the border with egg wash. Fold the pastry over to make a triangle or rectangle. Press the edges firmly with a fork to seal, a poor seal opens in the oven and the filling runs out. Brush the tops with egg wash and scatter {{demerara-sugar}} demerara sugar over if using. Cut two small slits in the top of each turnover to vent steam.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Bake on lined baking trays for 22 to 25 minutes, until the pastry is a deep golden-brown and the layers have visibly opened. The base should be crisp, check by lifting one with a palette knife. Serve warm or at room temperature on the day of baking; rough puff softens overnight.
